evals.prompt: Set up a minimal Duende IdentityServer with in-memory stores. Define one API scope called 'api1' and one machine-to-machine client called 'm2m.client' using client_credentials grant with the secret 'secret'. Make sure the middleware pipeline is in the correct order.
evals.expected_output: Program.cs with AddIdentityServer calling AddInMemoryApiScopes and AddInMemoryClients, a Config class or inline definitions with the API scope and client, and the correct middleware ordering: UseStaticFiles, UseRouting, UseIdentityServer, UseAuthorization.
assertions: Program.cs calls builder.Services.AddIdentityServer() to register IdentityServer services in DI
Program.cs calls AddInMemoryApiScopes with a scope named 'api1'
Program.cs calls AddInMemoryClients with a client having ClientId 'm2m.client' and AllowedGrantTypes set to ClientCredentials
Program.cs calls app.UseIdentityServer() in the middleware pipeline before app.UseAuthorization()
Program.cs calls app.UseRouting() before app.UseIdentityServer()

evals.prompt: I have an existing IdentityServer project and need to fix the middleware pipeline. Currently the order is: UseIdentityServer, UseRouting, UseAuthentication, UseAuthorization. What's wrong and what's the correct order?
evals.expected_output: Explanation that UseRouting must come before UseIdentityServer, that UseAuthentication is redundant because UseIdentityServer includes it internally, and the correct order is UseStaticFiles, UseRouting, UseIdentityServer, UseAuthorization.
assertions: Response explains that UseRouting() must come before UseIdentityServer() in the pipeline
Response explains that UseAuthentication() is redundant because UseIdentityServer() already calls it internally
Response shows the correct ordering: UseRouting before UseIdentityServer before UseAuthorization
Response mentions that UseAuthorization() must not be omitted as it is required by the Duende UI template

evals.prompt: Integrate ASP.NET Identity with my IdentityServer project. I have an ApplicationUser class and an ApplicationDbContext already set up. Show me how to wire up both ASP.NET Identity and IdentityServer together.
evals.expected_output: Program.cs with AddIdentity<ApplicationUser, IdentityRole> with AddEntityFrameworkStores and AddDefaultTokenProviders, followed by AddIdentityServer().AddAspNetIdentity<ApplicationUser>(). Explanation that AddAspNetIdentity registers IProfileService and other IdentityServer-specific services.
assertions: Program.cs calls AddIdentity<ApplicationUser, IdentityRole>() with AddEntityFrameworkStores<ApplicationDbContext>() to register ASP.NET Identity
Program.cs calls AddIdentityServer() followed by .AddAspNetIdentity<ApplicationUser>() to bridge ASP.NET Identity to IdentityServer
Response explains that AddAspNetIdentity registers IProfileService for adding claims to tokens
Program.cs includes AddDefaultTokenProviders() on the ASP.NET Identity registration

evals.prompt: My IdentityServer is deployed behind an nginx reverse proxy. The discovery document shows http:// URLs instead of https://, and my cookies are missing the secure attribute. How do I fix this?
evals.expected_output: Configuration of ForwardedHeadersOptions with XForwardedHost and XForwardedProto, adding UseForwardedHeaders() early in the pipeline before UseIdentityServer(). Explanation of the ASPNETCORE_FORWARDEDHEADERS_ENABLED environment variable as an alternative.
assertions: Response configures ForwardedHeadersOptions with ForwardedHeaders including XForwardedProto to restore the original HTTPS scheme
Response calls UseForwardedHeaders() early in the pipeline, before UseIdentityServer()
Response mentions the ASPNETCORE_FORWARDEDHEADERS_ENABLED=true environment variable as a simpler alternative for cloud/Kubernetes
Response explains that the reverse proxy strips the original scheme and host headers, causing IdentityServer to generate HTTP URLs in discovery

evals.prompt: Configure the IdentityServerOptions for my project. I need to set the issuer URI to https://identity.mycompany.com, load the license key from configuration, and emit scopes as a space-delimited string in JWTs. Also configure data protection for a multi-instance deployment using Entity Framework.
evals.expected_output: Program.cs with AddIdentityServer options block setting IssuerUri, LicenseKey from configuration, EmitScopesAsSpaceDelimitedStringInJwt=true, and AddDataProtection with PersistKeysToDbContext and SetApplicationName.
assertions: Program.cs sets options.IssuerUri = 'https://identity.mycompany.com' inside the AddIdentityServer options lambda
Program.cs loads the license key from configuration via builder.Configuration rather than hardcoding it
Program.cs sets options.EmitScopesAsSpaceDelimitedStringInJwt = true
Program.cs calls builder.Services.AddDataProtection() with PersistKeysToDbContext or equivalent persistent storage
Program.cs calls SetApplicationName on the data protection builder to prevent key isolation across instances

evals.prompt: I have a custom IUserClaimsPrincipalFactory<ApplicationUser> called CustomClaimsPrincipalFactory. I need to register it with ASP.NET Identity and IdentityServer so that my custom claims are included in tokens. Also, I need to disable tokens for inactive users by overriding IsActiveAsync.
evals.expected_output: Program.cs registering the custom factory before AddAspNetIdentity, and a CustomProfileService class inheriting from ProfileService<ApplicationUser> that overrides IsUserActiveAsync.
assertions: Program.cs registers CustomClaimsPrincipalFactory as IUserClaimsPrincipalFactory<ApplicationUser> BEFORE calling AddAspNetIdentity
Program.cs calls AddAspNetIdentity<ApplicationUser>() which will wrap and call through to the custom factory
A CustomProfileService class inherits from ProfileService<ApplicationUser> and overrides IsUserActiveAsync
The IsUserActiveAsync override checks a property on the user (e.g., user.IsEnabled or user.IsActive) to determine if the user is active
The CustomProfileService is registered in DI as the IProfileService implementation

evals.prompt: I'm setting up IdentityServer from scratch. What hosting pattern should I use? My team wants to co-host it with our main API to reduce deployment complexity. Also, should I set the IssuerUri explicitly or let it be inferred?
evals.expected_output: Recommendation to use a separate dedicated host for IdentityServer, explaining the security risks of co-hosting with an API. Explanation that IssuerUri should generally be left to infer from the request URL unless there is a specific need like internal Kubernetes addresses.
assertions: Response recommends a separate dedicated host for IdentityServer rather than co-hosting with the API
Response explains that co-hosting with an API increases the attack surface and creates conflicting middleware needs
Response advises against explicitly setting IssuerUri and recommends letting it be inferred from request URLs by default
Response explains that setting IssuerUri manually is only needed when IdentityServer is accessed on a different address than the expected issuer (e.g., internal Kubernetes address)

evals.prompt: Set up a complete IdentityServer project with identity resources (openid, profile), an API scope 'weatherapi', and an interactive web client using authorization_code grant. The client should have redirect URI https://webapp.example.com/signin-oidc and post-logout redirect URI https://webapp.example.com/signout-callback-oidc.
evals.expected_output: Program.cs with AddIdentityServer, AddInMemoryIdentityResources (openid, profile), AddInMemoryApiScopes (weatherapi), AddInMemoryClients with an interactive client configured for authorization_code with PKCE, and proper middleware pipeline.
assertions: Program.cs calls AddInMemoryIdentityResources with at least IdentityResources.OpenId() and IdentityResources.Profile()
Program.cs calls AddInMemoryApiScopes with a scope named 'weatherapi'
Program.cs defines a client with AllowedGrantTypes set to Code (authorization code with PKCE)
The client configuration includes RedirectUris containing 'https://webapp.example.com/signin-oidc' and PostLogoutRedirectUris containing 'https://webapp.example.com/signout-callback-oidc'
Program.cs has the correct middleware pipeline ordering: UseIdentityServer after UseRouting and before UseAuthorization
